title: Charmed Life — 3 (Fragment 3)
sourceText:
“Oh, I like this!” he said to Miss Bessemer.

“I’m afraid your bathroom is down the passage,” said Miss Bessemer, as if this was a drawback. But, as Cat had never had a private bathroom before, he did not mind in the least.

As soon as Miss Bessemer had gone, he hastened along to have a look at it. To his awe, there were three sizes of red towel and a sponge as big as a melon. The bath had feet like a lion’s. One corner of the room was tiled, and with red rubber curtains, for a shower. Cat could not resist experimenting. The bathroom was rather wet by the time he had finished. He went back to his room, a little damp himself. His trunk and box were there by this time, and a maid with red hair was unpacking them. She told Cat her name was Mary and wanted to know if she was putting things in the right places. She was perfectly pleasant, but Cat was very shy of her. The red hair reminded him of Miss Larkins, and he could not think what to say to her.

“Er—may I go down and have some tea?” he stammered.

“Please yourself,” she said—rather coldly, Cat thought. He ran downstairs again, feeling he might have gotten off on the wrong foot with her.

Gwendolen’s trunk was standing in the middle of her room. Gwendolen herself was sitting in a very queenly way at a round table by the window, with a big pewter teapot in front of her, a plate of brown bread and butter, and a plate of biscuits.

“I told the girl I’d unpack for myself,” she said. “I’ve got secrets in my trunk and my box. And I asked her to bring tea at once because I’m starving. And just look at it! Did you ever see anything so dull? Not even jam!”

“Perhaps the biscuits are nice,” Cat said hopefully. But they were not, or not particularly.

“We shall starve, in the midst of luxury!” sighed Gwendolen.

Her room was certainly luxurious. The wallpaper seemed to be made of blue velvet. The top and bottom of the bed was upholstered like a chair, in blue velvet with buttons in it, and the blue velvet bedspread matched it exactly. The chairs were painted gold. There was a dressing table fit for a princess, with little golden drawers, gold-backed brushes, and a long oval mirror surrounded by a gilded wreath. Gwendolen admitted that she liked the dressing table, though she was not so sure about the wardrobe, which had painted garlands and maypole dancers on it.

“It’s to hang clothes in, not to look at,” she said. “It distracts me. But the bathroom is lovely.”

The bathroom was tiled with blue and white tiles, and the bath was sunk down into the tiled floor. Over it, draped like a baby’s cradle, were blue curtains for when you wanted a shower. The towels matched the tiles. Cat preferred his own bathroom, but that may have been because he had to spend rather a long time in Gwendolen’s. Gwendolen locked him in it while she unpacked. Through the hiss of the shower—Gwendolen had only herself to blame that she found her bathroom thoroughly soaked afterwards—Cat heard her voice raised in annoyance at someone who had come in to take the dull tea away and caught her with her trunk open. When Gwendolen finally unlocked the bathroom door, she was still angry.

words.definition: A disadvantage or problem that makes something less attractive or effective.
words.example: The bathroom was down the passage, which was considered a drawback.
words.exampleText: “I’m afraid your bathroom is down the passage,” said Miss Bessemer, as if this was a drawback.

words.definition: Belonging to or for the use of one particular person or group of people only.
words.example: Cat had never had a private bathroom before.
words.exampleText: Cat had never had a private bathroom before, he did not mind in the least.

words.definition: To move or act quickly.
words.example: He hastened along to have a look at it.
words.exampleText: As soon as Miss Bessemer had gone, he hastened along to have a look at it.

words.definition: A feeling of great respect, admiration, or fear inspired by something majestic or powerful.
words.example: To his awe, there were three sizes of red towel and a big sponge.
words.exampleText: To his awe, there were three sizes of red towel and a sponge as big as a melon.

words.definition: To try something new or different to discover what effect it has or what result it produces.
words.example: Cat could not resist experimenting with the shower.
words.exampleText: Cat could not resist experimenting.

words.definition: Slightly wet, often in an unpleasant way.
words.example: He was a little damp himself after using the bathroom.
words.exampleText: The bathroom was rather wet by the time he had finished. He went back to his room, a little damp himself.

words.definition: The action of removing things from a bag, box, or suitcase after arriving somewhere.
words.example: A maid was unpacking the trunk and box.
words.exampleText: His trunk and box were there by this time, and a maid with red hair was unpacking them.

words.definition: To speak with sudden involuntary pauses and a tendency to repeat the initial letters of words.
words.example: He stammered when asking permission to have some tea.
words.exampleText: “Er—may I go down and have some tea?” he stammered.

words.definition: In an unfriendly or unemotional manner.
words.example: She said this rather coldly.
words.exampleText: “Please yourself,” she said—rather coldly, Cat thought.

words.word: gotten off on the wrong foot
words.ipa: ˈɡɒtən ɒf ɒn ðə rɒŋ fʊt
words.definition: To start something, especially a relationship or activity, badly.
words.example: He felt he might have gotten off on the wrong foot with her.
words.exampleText: He ran downstairs again, feeling he might have gotten off on the wrong foot with her.

words.definition: Having the qualities or manner befitting a queen; regal and dignified.
words.example: Gwendolen was sitting in a very queenly way.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen herself was sitting in a very queenly way at a round table by the window.

words.definition: A dull silver-coloured metal alloy used especially for making tableware.
words.example: There was a big pewter teapot on the table.
words.exampleText: ...with a big pewter teapot in front of her...

words.definition: Extremely hungry.
words.example: She said she was starving and asked for tea at once.
words.exampleText: I asked her to bring tea at once because I’m starving.

words.definition: Great comfort or elegance, especially involving great expense.
words.example: They would starve in the midst of luxury.
words.exampleText: We shall starve, in the midst of luxury!” sighed Gwendolen.

words.definition: A closely woven fabric of silk, cotton, or nylon with a thick short pile on one side.
words.example: The wallpaper seemed to be made of blue velvet.
words.exampleText: The wallpaper seemed to be made of blue velvet.

words.definition: Covered with a soft material, such as cloth or leather, used to cover furniture.
words.example: The bed was upholstered like a chair in blue velvet.
words.exampleText: The top and bottom of the bed was upholstered like a chair, in blue velvet with buttons in it.

words.definition: Covered thinly with gold leaf or gold paint.
words.example: The oval mirror was surrounded by a gilded wreath.
words.exampleText: ...a long oval mirror surrounded by a gilded wreath.

words.definition: A wreath of flowers and leaves worn on the head or hung as decoration.
words.example: The wardrobe had painted garlands and maypole dancers on it.
words.exampleText: ...the wardrobe, which had painted garlands and maypole dancers on it.

words.definition: To take someone's attention away from something.
words.example: The wardrobe distracted her.
words.exampleText: “It distracts me,” she said.

words.definition: Covered in or decorated with tiles.
words.example: One corner of the bathroom was tiled.
words.exampleText: One corner of the room was tiled.

words.definition: To become set or placed lower than the surrounding surface or level.
words.example: The bath was sunk down into the tiled floor.
words.exampleText: The bath was sunk down into the tiled floor.

words.definition: To arrange cloth loosely on or around something.
words.example: The blue curtains were draped like a baby’s cradle.
words.exampleText: Over it, draped like a baby’s cradle, were blue curtains for when you wanted a shower.

words.definition: Colours or patterns that go well together or are the same.
words.example: The towels matched the tiles.
words.exampleText: The towels matched the tiles.

words.definition: A feeling of being slightly angry or impatient.
words.example: Gwendolen’s voice was raised in annoyance.
words.exampleText: Cat heard her voice raised in annoyance at someone who had come in to take the dull tea away.

words.definition: Only and not involving anyone or anything else.
words.example: She was solely to blame for finding her bathroom soaked afterwards.
words.exampleText: Gwendolen had only herself to blame that she found her bathroom thoroughly soaked afterwards.
